step_by_step_thinking:
Recurrent bilateral optic neuritis along with transverse myelitis suggests an autoimmune etiology, which points towards neuromyelitis optica (NMO). The partial steroid response also supports this diagnosis as NMO patients often show variable responses to corticosteroids. 
The visual impairment noted does not align well with subacute combined degeneration or post-cerebral artery stroke presentations either. Neurosyphilis can cause similar symptoms but usually presents more chronically rather than recurrently over time.

raw_response:
Recurrent bilateral optic neuritis along with transverse myelitis suggests an autoimmune etiology, which points towards neuromyelitis optica (NMO). The partial steroid response also supports this diagnosis as NMO patients often show variable responses to corticosteroids. 
The visual impairment noted does not align well with subacute combined degeneration or post-cerebral artery stroke presentations either. Neurosyphilis can cause similar symptoms but usually presents more chronically rather than recurrently over time.

My Answer Choice: **Option A - Neuromyelitis Optica**. This condition fits best given both the recurrent nature of the disease involving multiple parts of the central nervous system like optic nerves and spinal cord, alongside the partially responsive pattern seen on treatment with steroids. Other conditions listed do not match these key features as closely.
